entries.title: The Wingman
entries.summary: Mark Kelly’s résumé and the goodwill he’s accrued as Gabby Giffords’s spouse have granted him a high level of admiration. Can it translate to excitement for a presidential campaign?
entries.content:
Mark Kelly’s résumé and the goodwill he’s accrued as Gabby Giffords’s spouse have granted him a high level of admiration. Can it translate to excitement for a presidential campaign?

“My wife, Gabby, was shot in the head.”

Obviously, I knew this. Senator Mark Kelly’s wife, the former congresswoman Gabby Giffords, was shot in the head and grievously wounded in a mass shooting outside a Tucson Safeway in 2011. It was one of the most notorious acts of political violence in America.

In several recent conversations with Kelly, however, I was struck by how often and how bluntly he repeated these exact words. Kelly, a retired Navy pilot and former NASA astronaut, seems to appreciate the curt repetition of the statement: My wife was shot in the head. It captures how stark and defining this incident was, and continues to be, in his life.

The near-assassination ended Giffords’s career in Congress and his in space. She was left with acute damage to the area of her brain that controls speech. Kelly was compelled by circumstance to create a new course for himself as a caregiver to his wife, Democratic senator, possible candidate for president, and severe critic of President Trump and his defense secretary, Pete Hegseth.

Fifteen years after the shooting, Kelly and Giffords remain a singular entity in American public life. I went to Arizona to join them at a series of events around Phoenix. We met at a coffee shop. It was a Friday afternoon, with triple-digit heat, and had already been a grind of a day for both of them. From our meeting, they would go to a reception, followed by a dinner, and then Giffords would fly to Louisiana, where she would attend a memorial service for eight children who’d been slaughtered in a mass shooting a few weeks earlier.

Interviewing Giffords and Kelly was like nothing I’ve ever experienced in 25 years of reporting on politics. In our time together, I kept snagging on the same inescapable question: How would their unparalleled dynamic play within the merciless spectacle of a presidential campaign?

“I’m Gabby.”

She says this by way of introduction, and as a wistful acknowledgment of her name’s cruel irony. Even by politician standards, Giffords was quite the chatterbox. In their joint memoir, Gabby: A Story of Courage, Love, and Resilience, Kelly estimates that before she was shot, Giffords did about 60 or 70 percent of the talking between them. Kelly was the reticent one in the couple, happy to let his wife gobble up the silences. The massacre left Giffords with a condition known as “non-fluent aphasia.” It does not affect intelligence, or ability to comprehend events or remember stories—only to relay them. After Tucson, Kelly says, he started carrying 95 percent of their conversations.

During my visit, Kelly often finished Giffords’s sentences and elaborated on what she was trying to say. She speaks in bursts of words and short fragments. It’s evident when she is trying to express something but her voice is not cooperating. She shakes her head, moves her mouth, rocks back and forth.

When we were first getting settled at the café, Kelly walked over with a blueberry muffin and a large cup of ice water.

“Drink your water!” Giffords declared. “China. Drink more water.”

What did this mean?

“You know what people say in China when you sneeze?” Kelly explained. “Drink more water.”

Giffords chuckled—which she does a lot. Kelly often joins her. They share a dry, absurdist, and understandably dark sense of humor. It’s a bit of a platitude to say this about political couples, but Kelly really does project as a different person around his spouse. His usual solo mode is quiet intensity. With Giffords present, he becomes much more engaged, as if he’s trying to draw out whatever he can of his wife’s former extroversion.

“They’re very symbiotic,” Senator Kirsten Gillibrand, a close friend of the couple who was elected to Congress with Giffords in 2006, told me. As an example, Gillibrand said that Giffords might throw out a single word to prompt Kelly to tell a whole story.

I watched this pattern several times. At one point, Kelly mentioned a trip that he and Giffords had taken to Western China early in their relationship.

“China,” Giffords said. “Gorilla. Monkey.”

“Oh, panda,” Kelly said, and took it from there. They had visited a panda preserve. For $80, visitors could be photographed holding a newborn.

“Then Gabby’s like, ‘I want to hold the baby panda,’” Kelly told me. A woman at the preserve told Kelly that for $35, she could offer a photograph with a less adorable panda. Kelly referred to it as a “raccoon,” but it was in fact a scruffy little red panda. Or “Budget Panda,” as Giffords called it.

“Danger, danger,” Giffords said.

“Oh yes, it’s endangered,” Kelly said. They still have the photo of Giffords holding Budget Panda.

“She has a smile on her face,” he said of Giffords. “But you know what she’s really thinking?”

“Fuck!” Giffords said, and they laughed again.

Giffords can be mischievous in what she encourages Kelly to say, sometimes leading him into impolitic stories. At one point, I asked Kelly whether he gets more questions about what it’s like to be a fighter pilot, an astronaut, or a senator.

“Oh, diapers,” Giffords interjected.

Kelly translated: “How do you go to the bathroom in space? I get a lot of those questions.”

“Skull,” Giffords said at another point.

“Yeah, we still got Gabby’s skull,” Kelly explained. “Like, her real skull.”

After she was shot, doctors performed an emergency craniectomy on Giffords, removing a large portion of her skull to reduce pressure on her swelling brain. Kelly asked the doctor what would happen to the original chunk of bone. “It will go in the incinerator,” the doctor told him. “And I was like, Gabby’s in a coma right now. I think she might want it.”

“We stuck it in the freezer,” Kelly told me—in a Tupperware container. They still have it too. “Next to the turkey,” Kelly said.

I thought this might be a joke, but it was not. Kelly seemed to notice me looking perhaps slightly taken aback. “It’s not in the freezer we use every day,” he clarified. “It’s a separate freezer.”

Here are some things that Mark Kelly has survived: a turbulent and sometimes violent childhood, 39 combat missions in Desert Storm, a missile blowing up just above his plane, and four space-shuttle voyages (two as mission commander). He has also braved the near-murder of the person he loves most and watched her get deluged with death threats for years afterward.

It is fair to observe that Pete Hegseth cannot scare him.

Kelly, who is 62, presents as a somewhat reserved, even sheepish, figure, which can belie the crowded and suspenseful life he has led—and the chilling conflict he now finds himself in with his own government. He also has a knack for understatement.

“I mean, it’s not great to wake up one morning and to find out the president wants you hanged,” Kelly said.

He was referring to a series of Truth Social posts from Trump last November. Kelly and five of his Democratic colleagues in the House and Senate had made a video reminding U.S. service members of their duty not to obey illegal orders. Trump responded by accusing the lawmakers of sedition and threatening to seek their execution. When I asked Kelly how he first learned of the president’s foreboding pronouncement, Kelly conceded that he did not in fact “wake up” to the news. He had been sitting next to another one of the video participants, his Senate colleague Elissa Slotkin of Michigan, during a mid-morning briefing on Venezuela. A security officer walked over and passed Slotkin a note from an aide.

“It says something like, ‘President called for your arrest,’” Kelly told me.

Slotkin left the room, came back a few minutes later, and informed Kelly that Trump had called for his arrest too. Slotkin confirmed this account to me. “I just slid the note over to him,” she said. “And we just had a whole conversation with our eyes.”

In addition to the president issuing a “punishable by DEATH!” decree via Truth Social, he also called for the video participants to be “ARRESTED AND PUT ON TRIAL.” Hegseth followed up with a letter of censure to Kelly, seeking to reduce his military rank and pension, and threatening “criminal prosecution.” (Of the lawmakers involved, only Kelly served more than 20 years in the armed forces, and thus has retired-military status and a continuing relationship with the Department of Defense.)

On a cloudy morning in early May, I sat with Kelly and Giffords in the back of a sedan headed to a federal courthouse in Washington. Kelly would be appearing before a panel of appeals-court judges in connection to a lawsuit he had filed against Hegseth to block his intended punishments.

“I think this is the first time in our country’s history that a president has ordered, or has called for, the execution of members of Congress,” Kelly told me.

This is the kind of historical distinction that you’d think would shake even the most jaded naval aviator. Yet during the short ride to the courthouse, he and Giffords appeared quite unbothered by any of this. Kelly spent much of the drive talking about how people often ask him his view on whether Pluto meets the criteria for being a full-size planet. The topic then shifted (as topics do) to the cryonically frozen head of the baseball legend Ted Williams, which is currently housed at the Alcor life-extension facility in Scottsdale, Arizona. “I’m Ted Williams’s head’s senator,” Kelly pointed out.

Kelly started reading headlines from his phone about an old controversy involving the allegedly shoddy treatment of the Splendid Splinter’s remains: “Williams’ Head Abused at Facility,” he declared merrily.

“It’s hard to get me nervous,” Kelly said as we arrived at the courthouse. “You think about, like, Trump saying he wants me hanged,” he told me later—“all right, I’ll deal with it.”

This was the inescapable subtext of pretty much everything we discussed over several hours of interviews: Adversity is extremely relative to Kelly. It is a notion that he internalized a long time ago, but especially after Giffords was shot.

If anything, being targeted by Trump and Hegseth appears more invigorating than ominous to Kelly. Not to be crass, but if you’re a Democrat, having Trump call for your execution can be excellent for business. Especially so if you are a Democrat who, like Kelly, is “seriously considering” a presidential run.

Since arriving to the Senate in 2020, Kelly’s storybook résumé and the goodwill he’s accrued as Giffords’s spouse have granted him a high level of admiration that has yet to translate to a high level of excitement for him as a potential standard-bearer. He was a finalist to be Kamala Harris’s running mate in 2024, but was passed over. He is seen as a serious and respected lawmaker, steeped in such matters as national defense and border security. But in his relatively brief time in Washington, he has struggled to acquire main-character status or a readily identifiable fight that could burnish the profile of an ascendent national candidate.

He sounds eager to be viewed as a cause célèbre. “This is not about me,” Kelly often insists, as politicians are wont to do (usually at events featuring themselves, and devoted to their career advancement).

At his May 7 court hearing, Kelly was trailed by about a dozen reporters and cameras, and a campaign-worthy entourage. After the session, which lasted about 90 minutes, he delivered a brief, ostentatiously defiant speech in front of the courthouse entrance on Constitution Avenue. “The secretary of defense picked the wrong guy,” Kelly said.

He was flanked by Giffords; his younger daughter, Claire (one of two grown kids from his first marriage); a former Navy colleague; and several other veterans and retired service members. The courthouse loomed directly behind him, and American flags flew overhead while, at the next building over, a massive likeness of Trump’s face dominated the front of the Labor Department’s headquarters.

“They’re trying to send a message to other retired veterans, and really to all of us,” Kelly said. “If you say something that the president, or this administration, does not like, they’re going to come after you.”

As much as Kelly might be milking this attention, being subjected to such a high-level threat can arouse serious menace from the acolyte mobs. After Trump’s posts about the lawmakers in the video, Slotkin told me that her father, in the last weeks of his life, was swatted in the middle of the night. Kelly said he received more death threats in a single day than he would typically get in several months. (For what it’s worth, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt later insisted that Trump in fact did not want the lawmakers to be executed.)

Kelly told me that he tries not to worry, because he is oriented to probabilities. “I’m a believer in math,” Kelly said. “So, when you look at Desert Storm, you look at all the combat missions flown, and the number of people killed—flying on the space shuttle is probably 10 times more dangerous.” And both, certainly, are much more dangerous than his current profession, he said. Statistically speaking, the universe of contemporary U.S. politicians who have actually taken a bullet is minuscule.

“But not in my family,” Kelly noted. “I thought I had the risky job—until January 8th of 2011.”

Giffords was scheduled to hold one of her regular “Congress on Your Corner” meetings with constituents in front of a Tucson Safeway that day. She had just been elected to her third term, even as Republicans had retaken control of the House in a torrent of Tea Party–driven anger over the Affordable Care Act. At about 10:10 a.m., a few minutes into the event, a gunman named Jared Lee Loughner opened fire.

Kelly was in Houston, training for a space-shuttle mission. He learned of the shooting in a call from one of Giffords’s staffers. As he flew to Tucson with his mother and his two teenage daughters, NPR reported that Giffords had died. Kelly, typically a non-demonstrative engineer, retreated to the airplane bathroom and sobbed. When he emerged 20 minutes later, he called the hospital and learned that Giffords was in fact still clutching to life. University Medical Center in Tucson corrected the media reports.

Giffords woke from a medically induced coma about a week later. Loughner’s bullet had ripped through the left hemisphere of her brain, entering over her left eye and exiting out the back of her skull. She could barely move, and could speak only a few words at a time, though it was clear that her brain worked well enough to know that something unimaginable had occurred. “What is happening to me?” she kept saying.

Kelly was diligent in documenting this period in which it all changed, taking video of her ordeal from close in. Giffords was hooked up to tubes and monitors, and her head was shaved. Some of the footage appears in the 2022 documentary Gabby Giffords Won’t Back Down. The video shows, more than anything visible today, Giffords’s despair. As happens with aphasia, she perseverates on random words. “Chicken, chicken, chicken” keeps tumbling out. At one point, she puts her head down and just cries.

The shooting scrambled each of their plans entirely. Although Kelly had already traveled multiple times into space and seemingly reached the pinnacle of his career, Giffords had very much been rising in hers. She was hailed as a natural politician, a performer from a young age who had starred in music and theater productions. (Gwyneth Paltrow is her second cousin.) She grew up in the Tanque Verde Valley, northeast of Tucson, and after college in California and a stint in New York, returned home to run her family business, El Campo Tire. She built her local celebrity as a motormouthed pitchwoman, appearing in TV ads in which she would order viewers to “Race into the El Campo Service Center today, before the offer is gone!” Giffords launched her first campaign in 2000, for the Arizona House of Representatives. Six years later, at 36, she won her U.S. House seat and was immediately touted as a future senator. She was a vibrant speaker, hawkish on border security, and, yes, a proud gun owner.

Kelly and Giffords met in 2003, on a trip to China for an international conference of young leaders. She was a state legislator, and he was at NASA. They reconnected at another conference, this one in Arizona, a year later, by which point Kelly was divorced and Giffords was single. In November 2004, Giffords was doing research for death-penalty-related legislation and invited Kelly to join her on a tour of an Arizona state prison in Florence. “We went to death row on our first date,” Kelly says. He recalls at one point stepping into the actual gas chamber.

Before long, Giffords started showing up to Kelly’s rocket launches in Houston. Kelly came to Giffords’s campaign events in Tucson. As political sidekicks went, Kelly jumped straight out of the catalog: the sturdy astronaut, content to toil in the wings with his intricate machinery. He sent her emails from space. “Tell my wife I love her very much; she knows,” Kelly said, quoting David Bowie’s “Space Oddity,” while appearing via video at a U2 concert, from the International Space Station.

Beyond the storybook optics, Giffords and Kelly were also well-matched workaholics with high-velocity careers. It was a commuter arrangement: After they were married, Kelly continued to live in Houston, while Giffords split time between Washington and Arizona. “We did not live together until the day she got shot,” Kelly told me.

Before the shooting, the couple had been trying to have children. Giffords, then 40, desperately wanted to become a mother and was undergoing fertility treatments at Bethesda Naval Hospital. She’d had an appointment to have an embryo implanted on Monday, January 10. After the shooting, that dream, too, quickly faded. She embarked instead on a prolonged and agonizing rehabilitation.

In addition to her difficulty speaking, Giffords was left with severely limited peripheral vision in one eye, and suffered partial paralysis and motor impairment on the right side of her body. She uses a walking stick, sometimes a wheelchair for longer distances. Flights can be difficult. She travels with a full-time health aide. Yet Giffords keeps a punishing schedule, much of it connected to her anti-gun-violence organization. She meets with victims and families, attends vigils and funerals, and spends more than half of her time on the road.

Periodically, Giffords breaks into song. Although the speech part of her brain was ravaged, the music area remains intact, Kelly told me. Lyrics flow more easily for her than words.

“The sun will come out tomorrow,” she sang on the topic of the Democrats’ prospects in the midterms this fall. “Bet your bottom dollar that tomorrow, come what may!” On other occasions, when I referred to something that had occurred the day before, Giffords would chime in with, “Yesterday, all my troubles seemed so far away.”

“Yesterday” and “Tomorrow” are Giffords’s go-to refrains. Her life with Kelly carries a hard line of demarcation between the before and the after.

Kelly grew up in West Orange, New Jersey, in an Irish Catholic household of little outward affection and high tumult. Richard and Patricia Kelly—both police officers—were at constant odds. “One of the worst feelings in the world is, you’re in a deep sleep and then you slowly wake up to your parents screaming at one another and throwing things,” Scott Kelly, Mark’s identical twin brother, told me. “We were raised in that environment of, you know, a lot of cops, alcohol, some violence, definitely a lot of drunkenness.” The twins’ general approach, for much of their youth, was to try to protect their mother as best they could and stay occupied outside the house. After getting their driver’s licenses, the Kelly boys worked as EMTs, rushing in ambulances to the scenes of shootings, stabbings, and car crashes.

Mark said he never realized there was anything unusual about his upbringing. “When you’re a kid, that’s your experience,” he told me. “So, you’re not so sure, like, Is this normal or not normal? And you realize later it’s not normal.”

Kelly described his father as an alcoholic, but one who did not drink every day and rarely at home: “It was, like, a Friday, Saturday-night thing at the bar with his cop friends.” In some ways, Kelly added, he looked up to his father. Richard Kelly coached his sons’ Little League teams, worked as a detective, and “was putting bad guys in jail.” He also investigated mobsters, Mark told me. (New Jersey in the ’70s and ’80s, go figure.) With some pride, Kelly noted that in The Sopranos, Tony went to West Orange High School.

In his memoir with Giffords, Kelly writes that he and Scott were “borderline juvenile delinquents” growing up, always getting into trouble, accidents, and fights. They were late academic bloomers. Mark was the first to become captivated by a career in outer space; his twin, younger by six minutes, became an astronaut as well. Mark had no interest in politics, let alone a career in it. He wanted to pursue the moon, not the swamp.

But after the horror of January 2011 and Giffords’s forced retirement, Kelly began to get mentioned as a potential political candidate himself. He first ran for Senate in 2020 to fill the seat vacated by John McCain’s death in 2018; he defeated Republican Martha McSally, and was reelected to a full six-year term in 2022. I asked Kelly what had made him decide to run for office. First off, he said, he was concerned about “the trajectory of our country with Donald Trump in the White House.” This did not exactly make him a brave and lonely voice in the Democratic wilderness. But Kelly traces his particular aha moment to a get-out-the-vote rally that he attended in Phoenix before the 2018 midterms. A woman, probably in her 30s, approached him.

“She just stands next to me and says, ‘Hey, my son has Down Syndrome. And I’m terrified he’s going to lose his health insurance. Would you please consider running for the U.S. Senate?’” Kelly told the woman he would think about it.

As political origin stories go, this one feels quite labored. It seemed dubious to me, given that Kelly’s name had come up in the past, that it had never occurred to him to run for something until a woman he met at a GOTV rally brought it up. “I don’t know if I wanted to be in the Senate badly enough,” he told me. “But when I focus on something, I work really hard at it.”

Kelly makes frequent cracks about the astonishing sequence of events that landed him in his current peacocking profession. Before a taped interview at a Phoenix TV studio, he pulled out a makeup brush and applied foundation to dull the shine of his bald head. “I used to be the commander of the space shuttle,” he said, shaking his head. “Now I carry my own makeup.”

After the interview, Kelly headed off on a visit to the John S. McCain III VFW Post 7401, a half hour away in Chandler. When we arrived, he spent about 10 minutes serviceably glad-handing his way through a front bar area, dressed in an old flight jacket and camouflage cap with NAVY in big print across the front. At least two of the people Kelly met urged him to run for president. He said he’d not yet decided, though he is taking many of the so-called necessary steps, including traveling to South Carolina, Nevada, and New Hampshire in recent months.

His speeches need work. Kelly’s voice is more conversational than booming. His words sync awkwardly with robotic hand motions. When Kelly tries to amplify, it sounds like he’s straining. He’s used to working in tight places rather than big rooms.

At the VFW, Cindy McCain, the widow of the late senator, introduced Kelly. He moved quickly to his skirmish with the administration. “I said something the president didn’t like,” he said. “Anybody see this?” Nods and scattered applause across the room. “And the president said I should be hanged, executed, prosecuted,” Kelly said. He paused for effect. “The guy couldn’t even get the order right!”

Kelly’s main message was grounded in the old soldiers’ credo: that “to fight for a cause larger than yourself,” as McCain used to say, was its own reward. It was a friendly audience. He took questions, and came well briefed and well versed in legislative detail.

One question, however, exposed a weakness. A woman named Jennifer Worthy told a gutting story about her husband, Marine Sergeant Joseph Worthy, who was battling Stage 4 colon cancer. He had fought in Desert Storm, where he was almost certainly exposed to lethal toxins from burning oil fields. At the end of her question, Jennifer choked up. Her husband, she said, was getting chemotherapy treatment right at that hour. She asked Kelly if he would take a picture with her that she could bring back. He nodded yes, and the crowd broke into applause as Jennifer wiped away more tears.

For a politician, the instinctive move in this situation is easy: hug the woman. She was standing about 10 feet away and clearly in crisis. It should not take an elite pain-feeler like Bill Clinton to recognize this. But Kelly came off oddly oblivious. He stood in place and kept glancing around the room. He retreated into his sound bites and talked about himself: how treacherous it was for him to fly missions during the Gulf War after Saddam Hussein’s forces set fire to the oil fields. “I was there on an aircraft carrier, the USS Midway,” he said. “I’m flying around there, and the sky is black.” (Kelly did pose for a photo with Worthy and had a brief visit with her after his remarks.)

When we spoke later, Kelly acknowledged that his political personality is a work in progress. His background, he says, sets him apart from that of most other senators. In a body dominated by lawyers and debate-team types, he “brings the training of an engineer, the clarity of a tactician from his time as a naval aviator, and the cool-under-pressure of an astronaut,” his Democratic colleague Jon Ossoff, of Georgia, told me. Kelly excels in quiet, stressful, and often collaborative environments—like a spacecraft or, as Ossoff said, the Senate Intelligence Committee, where he and Kelly serve together.

Still, it’s hard to imagine a successful presidential candidate that doesn’t possess at least some knack for the performative conviviality required of big-ticket politicians.

“In my defense,” Kelly told me, “if you look at people that are, like, the normal stereotypical engineers on, like, a spectrum”—he made a hand motion, presumably to convey a spectrum of personality types—he grades himself as more gregarious than most. “I’m much more comfortable now giving random people hugs,” Kelly said.

By contrast, Kelly told me at the coffee shop in Phoenix, Giffords is always giving random people hugs—whether or not they want to be hugged.

“Airport,” Giffords said.

“She gives a lot of hugs at the airport,” Kelly confirmed. He turned to her.

“Well, I mean, you just kissed a dude on his face, like, an hour ago,” he said. “Who was that guy?”

He nudged his wife and shook his head. She smiled.

The three of us spoke for a while about the brutality of politics these days. How do they tolerate it?

“Servant, servant, servant,” Giffords said.

“You can’t live your life paranoid,” Kelly added.

I took this to mean, basically, that they are servants to a cause greater than themselves. And it comes with occupational hazards they both know too well.

“Gabby, do you worry about somebody else coming up to you and shooting you?” Kelly asked her. She shook her head.

“I mean, if somebody decided to walk up to me and shoot me, not much I can do about it,” Kelly said. “Space shuttle blows up? Not much I can do about it.”

“So sad,” Giffords said.

It’s easy to sense an undercurrent of Kelly wanting to excel in politics in order to realize the full ambitions of his wife. He talks a lot these days about how he will not be “silenced” by Hegseth, and how he is standing up for others in doing so. (“There are 2 million retired service members like me that they want to silence,” he says repeatedly.) One parallel Kelly does not draw explicitly, but that is evident to anyone who is familiar with Giffords’s story, is that he will not be silenced by the defense secretary just as his wife would not be silenced by her would-be assassin. “Mr. Loughner, you may have put a bullet through her head, but you haven’t put a dent in her spirit,” Kelly told the gunman in 2012 at his sentencing hearing. (Loughner is serving seven consecutive life terms in prison, plus an additional 140 years tacked on for the victims he injured and maimed.)

Listening to Kelly and Giffords, I was periodically struck by how their endeavors have been interwoven. “Certainly, if I was not married to Gabby, I would not be sitting here,” Kelly told me during one of our conversations in his Senate office. “And if she wasn’t shot, I mean, I wouldn’t be here, right?”

I wasn’t entirely sure at first what point Kelly was making. That if he hadn’t ever been attached to Giffords, he would not have been elected on his own? Or that most people know him only as a grieving spouse, and he was elected out of sympathy? When I asked Kelly to elaborate, he explained that if not for the shooting, he thinks his wife would have eventually been elected to the Senate. But because that was not possible for her, being Giffords’s husband had afforded him the statewide name recognition and political organization to do it himself.

Our last stop of the day in Arizona was a fundraising dinner for the state Democratic Party, officially christened the “Gabby Giffords Gala.” Kelly and Giffords headlined the program, which included a procession of local elected officials in front of about 1,000 party activists and appendages. In keeping with their professions, Kelly and Giffords have to do a lot of these banquets. They usually stand onstage together, and Kelly does most of the talking. Giffords might say a few words at the end, usually halting phrases:

“I chose to make a new start—to move ahead.”

“Do not look back.”

“Setbacks are hard.”

“I tell myself, move ahead.”

Giffords received her customary standing ovation at the banquet, along with chants of “Gabby, Gabby, Gabby.” Kelly then helped her down from the podium.

Kelly and Giffords are an inspiring couple to be around, and also not an easy one. A special kind of burden goes with being the surviving protagonists of a national tragedy. In general, when you say you’re running for president, the feel-good story ends. If we’ve learned anything over the past 10 years, it is the extraordinary capacity of people to be cruel in politics.

Kelly told me that he goes onto social media sometimes. He forces himself to read comment sections. “It thickens your skin a little bit,” he explained. “I see what they say.” I wondered how he would handle a political adversary going after his wife—calling her names, saying heartless things about her disability, deploying the kind of hateful language that Trump has empowered many of his supporters to use. It’s easy to imagine.

I asked Kelly, straight up, if he thought America was ready to have a disabled first lady.

“Hey,” he said, “in my opinion, we have a disabled president right now.”

He pivoted back to Giffords: how much of a role model she is, with all she has overcome. “The way she connects with people is unlike anybody else I’ve ever known,” Kelly said. “Before she was injured, people used to compare her with Bill Clinton.” His wife, he told me, can handle herself. “She’s been in this business,” he said. “And she had, you know, like the worst thing that any politician could have happen, right? She gets shot in the head.”

Kelly keeps his official letter of censure from Hegseth taped to the door of his Senate office. I hadn’t noticed it until he mentioned it to me. The document—dated January 5, 2026—is addressed to “Captain Kelly” and printed on “Department of War” stationery. It is three pages long, one of them crooked. Kelly told me that he hung the missive sloppily on purpose, to reflect his lack of respect for its contents and sender. Perhaps, he said, he should smear it with ketchup.

I asked Kelly why he displays the letter on his door.

“I think that, you know, the seriousness of this whole thing,” he said. “It’s good for me to be reminded of what’s at stake, and who these people are.”

Politically at least, it also can be good to remind as many people as possible that the Trump administration is targeting Mark Kelly. Kelly raised $12.5 million in the last quarter of 2025, much of it after November 24, when the Defense Department announced that it was investigating “serious allegations of misconduct” by the lawmakers in the “illegal orders” video. Kelly brought up his lawsuit against the defense secretary at every public event of his that I attended. (The panel of three D.C. judges has yet to announce a decision, although two of them signaled strong opposition to punishing Kelly at the May 7 hearing.) He always took care to identify the case by its docket name, Kelly v. Hegseth. I once referred to the litigants in the wrong order—Hegseth v. Kelly—and Kelly immediately corrected me.

In Phoenix, I asked Giffords the obligatory question: Does she want Kelly to run for president?

“All of it,” she said. “Yeah, go for it.”

“We’re a team” is another mantra of hers.

Scott Kelly told me that he also wants his brother to run in 2028—and not just because then Mark could become the first identical-twin astronaut ever to reach the White House (unless I’m overlooking someone). “I’ve known him my whole life,” Scott said. “He doesn’t have the type of ego that he would think he can fix every problem and would be the savior to this country.” On the contrary, his brother is low-key, collaborative, and analytical. This might be precisely what the country is primed for after the Trump experience, Scott said.

During an office interview with Kelly in June, I asked him for an update on his thinking about running for president. His official position remained “considering it.” But he also made the point, in a variety of ways, that once he gets locked into an idea, he tends to go ahead and launch it. I took this to mean he is more likely than not to run.

As we were discussing this decision, Kelly talked about the experience of getting close to being Harris’s running mate in 2024. He clearly wanted it, more so than passed-over running mates usually admit. He was one of two runners-up—along with Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro—for the job that eventually went to Minnesota Governor Tim Walz.

In her campaign memoir, 107 Days, Harris lists a few concerns that she had about Kelly. One was that he had never experienced an “oh shit moment” in his political career. This is quite a remarkable statement. Harris added that she “couldn’t afford to test Mark Kelly in that ugly grinder” of a national campaign.

Kelly told me that he’d only “read parts” of Harris’s memoir. Presumably, these were the parts that corresponded to mentions of “Kelly, Mark.” He smiled when I suggested this and did not deny it. When I brought up that Harris said she was concerned that he wasn’t tested as a national candidate, Kelly grew more openly annoyed than I might have expected, given how beside the point the topic is now.

“What does that mean?” Kelly said. “I ran one of the most competitive Senate races in the country, in a swing state,” he continued. “My Senate races were harder than her Senate races.”

He also noted, sarcastically, that some people apparently found it charming that Walz asked for Diet Mountain Dew to drink during his VP interview. “Shapiro and I asked for water,” he said, smirking.

As I spent time around Kelly, I concluded that he would have been a good choice for Harris—and not just because he’d probably have done less harm than Walz did. Kelly struck me as having an ideal vice-presidential temperament. My question, then, is whether he could plausibly sell himself as the ringmaster in the chaotic circus of a modern presidency. In this age of infinite spotlights, people are obsessed with finding “alphas.” It helps to be a showman, and not to be boring. Trump is proof of how far that can get a person—and also where it can lead the country.

One last note on Harris’s running-mate decision: The former vice president wrote that another concern she had was that she did not want to subject Kelly’s military record to the kind of scrutiny, or smears, that presidential campaigns can invite. This packed a certain richness, given how Walz’s military record—or his overstatement of it—created its own recurring migraine for Harris. But when I asked about this, Kelly shifted to his current predicament. “I would be ecstatic if Pete Hegseth managed to get all of my military records and stick it online,” he told me.

He would go further. “Mark has made it clear to me that he is willing to be in prison to defend the Constitution,” Ossoff told me.

Kelly made this clear to me too. If “they actually sent me to prison, I’ll be fine,” he told me. “I’ll continue to fight back against these people.”

I mentioned that it would seem unlikely at this point that Kelly would be sent to jail over the “illegal orders” video. The government, after all, already failed once to win an indictment against the lawmakers. Kelly agreed, but then went on to describe scenarios in which it could happen: If, Kelly suggested, his lawsuit against Hegseth goes to the Supreme Court and they rule against him, the Pentagon could then have other punishment options. “I could wind up in a nonjudicial punishment process in the Navy,” he said. “And you know, that’s a big pain in the ass.”

“I tell you what they could do; they could try to indict us again,” Kelly continued. “Then, maybe do it in a place where maybe they get enough grand jurors. And then I wind up getting charged with seditious conspiracy.”

I joked that if Kelly did wind up in prison, he would probably raise $50 million within a week. He reiterated that he was not scared of imprisonment. His wife is not worried about it either, he said.

“We’ve been through a lot,” Kelly told me. “And you know, if they lock me up, I’m sure she’d come visit me.”

entries.title: What’s Behind the Rise in Mild Autism?
entries.summary: The number of people with the condition has increased rapidly in recent decades. Some researchers and clinicians worry that many patients have been wrongly diagnosed.
entries.content:
The number of people with the condition has increased rapidly in recent decades. Some researchers and clinicians worry that many patients have been wrongly diagnosed.

Not long ago, autism was considered rare, but now it’s fairly common: Autism diagnoses in children have increased nearly fivefold across the United States in the past couple of decades, from one in every 150 8-year-olds in 2000 to one in every 31 in 2022. Adult diagnoses have also gone up rapidly in recent years.

According to many researchers, a significant portion of this growth comes from a rise in diagnoses of milder forms of autism—cases in which people may have a normal to high IQ and typical speaking abilities but struggle with social interactions or have intense interests. Severe autism, in contrast, is generally associated with low IQ, little or no verbal speech, and a probability that the person will need some form of lifelong care or supervision. A study published last year found that the prevalence of autism with “mild, borderline, or no significant adaptive challenges” increased from 2000 to 2016, but that the frequency of cases involving “moderate to profound challenges” decreased slightly. And the likelihood that an autistic person also has an intellectual disability has declined meaningfully since the 1980s, from about 70 percent to about 30 percent.

I spoke with five researchers and clinicians who say they’re concerned about the possibility that too many people are being diagnosed with mild autism. They say that some individuals who have received these diagnoses might instead have anxiety or another mental illness. Others might not have any diagnosable mental-health condition, and may be incorrectly perceiving their typical social struggles as a sign that they have a disorder. “Some of those people absolutely have autism,” Catherine Lord, a clinical psychologist at UCLA who specializes in autism, told me. But some people who think, “Maybe this is the answer to why I feel different or I’m not happy,” may simply be mistaken, she said. This overdiagnosis trend, these experts say, can hurt both people incorrectly classified as mildly autistic and those with more severe forms of autism.

To be sure, researchers don’t know what the average expected number of autism cases in a given population should be. Generally, to be considered autistic, a person must have exhibited social difficulties and repetitive behaviors—such as hand-flapping and spinning—from before the age of 5. But no brain scan or blood test can diagnose the condition, so clinicians must rely on a person’s own account—or their parent’s account—of their challenges. A recent study found that nearly half of the children who had been given an autism diagnosis in one clinic did not meet the criteria for autism when they were reevaluated in another clinic. Diagnoses can be especially off for kids with visual or hearing impairments or other disabilities. In adults, autism diagnoses are inaccurate about 20 percent of the time, even with rigorous tests, Lord said.

Some of the inconsistency in diagnosis can be explained by the constraints of the health-care system: Ideally, psychiatrists and psychologists take into account interviews with a patient’s teachers and parents—or, in the case of adults, their siblings and friends. But clinicians may not have the time to devote that much attention to their patients, and they don’t want to miss any cases. Lord said that many professionals therefore diagnose patients based solely on the patient’s stated symptoms or history. The diagnostic criteria, too, are somewhat unclear: Bad eye contact, for example, can be a symptom of autism, but it can also be a sign of schizophrenia, ADHD, social anxiety, or depression, Eric Fombonne, a professor emeritus of psychiatry at Oregon Health and Science University, told me. The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders states that autism causes “impairments,” but what constitutes an impairment can be subject to interpretation.

Some of the experts I spoke with also told me that more people seem to be seeking out an autism diagnosis now than they did in the past. Having a precise label for social challenges or feelings of alienation can feel freeing and relieving. Unlike, say, those for schizophrenia or bipolar disorder, an autism diagnosis has become desirable to some people, Uta Frith, an emeritus psychology professor at University College London who was one of the first people to study mild autism, told me. She said that there’s been “a big increase in the demand for the diagnosis.” According to Fombonne, autism “has become chic.” Lord said that she, too, has seen more patients asking whether they should be diagnosed with autism, and many of them have relatively mild symptoms. Lord gave an example of a type of person she sees who has a high IQ, may be depressed or anxious, and is questioning their gender or sexuality. “So somebody really trying to figure themselves out,” she said. They “may be somewhat socially awkward, often have friends, but feel like there’s something missing.”

Content about autism on social media may be inspiring some people to seek out these diagnoses. Influencers have flooded TikTok and Instagram with videos describing supposed “hidden” symptoms of autism, such as intensely enjoying something you’re interested in and looking up the menu of a restaurant before you go. These videos suggest that you might be autistic if you notice subtle communication styles, dislike small talk (even if you’re good at it), or go indoors when it’s too hot outside. Social media has also promoted the idea that many autistic people aren’t diagnosed until later in life because they “mask,” or camouflage their autistic traits. In her book The Age of Diagnosis, the British neurologist Suzanne O’Sullivan describes a woman named Poppy who was given an autism diagnosis in her early 20s. O’Sullivan writes that looking at TikTok crystallized Poppy’s sense that she probably was autistic. “I wanted to know if there were other people like me out there, so I went on TikTok and I found that there were lots,” Poppy said, according to O’Sullivan.

Perhaps unsurprising, one study found that only about a quarter of the most popular videos about autism on TikTok were accurate. Nevertheless, a correct diagnosis of mild autism can be beneficial. Donna Henderson, a Maryland neuropsychologist who specializes in less severe forms of autism, told me, “There is just no bigger gift we can give to somebody than knowing who they are.” Most of her clients are not swayed by social media, she said. “The people who I’m seeing in my office are not 12-year-olds who saw one TikTok video and decided they were autistic. They’re people who have been thinking about this for a very long time.” Having a diagnosis, she added, can help individuals look back on past difficulties with more understanding and self-compassion, and prompt them to connect with neurodiverse people. Lord also said that people who find out that they have autism can get “access to other people who will be kind and sympathetic and want to hear about the things that are hard for them.”

Zoe Gross, a director at the Autistic Self Advocacy Network, pointed to other reasons that a formal diagnosis can help: It can unlock access to accommodations at school and at work, as well as protection from discrimination, in a way that simply being a little different would not. Gross, who was diagnosed when she was 4, told me that as a child, she had trouble connecting with other kids. Knowing she was autistic helped her understand why. Learning that you struggle “because you have a disability,” Gross said, “is really empowering.”

Still, many of the clinicians and researchers I spoke with said that inaccurate diagnoses can be a real problem. A child erroneously diagnosed with autism might be subjected to an intensive and time-consuming therapy called Applied Behavior Analysis for no reason. An improper diagnosis can trail a child through their school years, Fombonne said, but a different, underlying condition the person might have—such as depression and anxiety—might go untreated.

For adults, a faulty diagnosis can become a self-fulfilling prophecy, O’Sullivan said. Someone who considers themselves autistic when they aren’t might shrink away from new risks and challenges, for instance, telling themselves that a brain disorder prevents them from, say, taking on a leadership role at work or engaging in public speaking. “Once you’ve got the diagnosis, it’s very strongly implied that you can’t change,” Frith said. “Instead, the onus is on the environment to change.” (Gross disagreed with this. “Someone learning that they’re autistic is not gonna stop them from going out and acquiring new skills,” she said.) Lord said that a diagnosis might encourage “people to exaggerate sometimes the normal, ordinary reactions that some of us have to, for example, sensory things.” Rather than try to do the best they can despite their limitations, Lord said, they might be “focusing on how mistreated I am.”

Diagnostic inflation might also make finding the cause of autism, or discovering effective treatments, less likely, O’Sullivan told me. “As this community of people that we call autistic grows and becomes so much more diverse,” she said, “the chances that anyone will ever find a specific cause for it” diminishes, because the people being studied may be too different from one another for scientists to find many commonalities among them.

And overdiagnosing mild autism can make life harder for people on the most severe end of the spectrum. Those children and adults are “crowded out of services; they are crowded out of classrooms,” Alison Singer, the mother of a severely autistic adult child and president of the Autism Science Foundation, told me. Singer said that she hears from parents who say that they have trouble placing their severely autistic kids in autism-specific programs—swimming lessons, karate classes, and the like—because the slots have been taken up by kids with milder forms of autism.

An obvious solution to that problem would be for providers to offer enough assistance to meet the needs of everyone who has autism. Those needs may differ depending on whether a person has mild or severe autism, so perhaps the best way to think of the spectrum is as a collection of different conditions with different concerns, priorities, and treatments. Singer’s group has supported the creation of a “profound autism” label for autistic people who have a low IQ and minimal language abilities. People who struggle with socializing or sensory processing but require little support for daily life tasks used to be classified as having Asperger’s syndrome, a term that fell out of favor because of its namesake’s unsavory biography; a new name for the same condition might be in order. As it stands, a diagnostic category that encompasses both CEOs and people who cannot live independently may have outlived its usefulness.

entries.title: J. D. Vance’s New Book Wasn’t Meant to Be Read
entries.content:
J. D. Vance’s new book, Communion, answers a question no one had thought to ask: What would Augustine’s Confessions have looked like if the saint’s greatest literary influence had been Hillary Clinton’s immortal classic, Hard Choices?

One of the best-selling nonfiction titles of 2014, Hard Choices runs to more than 600 pages, many of which consist of factoids copied and pasted from Clinton’s old speeches. It is possibly the least introspective book ever written. Her ghostwriters’ modus operandi was to take a bunch of platitudinous abstractions from her time at the State Department (“The strategic fundamentals of our relationship—shared democratic values, economic imperatives and diplomatic priorities—are moving us closer to an understanding and a trust that reflects the convergence of values and interests”) and switch the tenses (“The strategic fundamentals of our relationship with India—shared democratic values, economic imperatives, and diplomatic priorities—were pushing both countries’ interests into closer convergence”).

Vance’s book never quite sinks to this level, but it does make me wonder why it was published, and for whose edification. Even by the standards of faith-driven airport nonfiction, Communion is not very interesting. Despite what the jacket copy suggests, the book is not “the story” of Vance’s conversion to Roman Catholicism, which had already been recounted in an essay published six years ago in the journal I edit, The Lamp. (Unaltered passages from it appear in the present volume without acknowledgment.) If anything, the book, from its Methodist-church-emblazoned cover on, seems oddly squeamish about the Catholic Church, couching most of its arguments concerning religious questions in a generically Christian register. When Vance does address specifically Catholic subject matter, his language is in some cases awkward and in almost all curiously unidiomatic. At one point, he describes himself attending “a Mass mourning Christ’s passion,” a phrase so theologically defective that it could be the subject of an entire ecumenical council.

Like Hard Choices, Communion is full of what might loosely be described as anecdotes. Take the one about Vance’s future wife, Usha, recommending Joan Didion’s memoir The Year of Magical Thinking while they were students at Yale Law School. Usha said she found it “cathartic,” prompting Vance to look up this unknown word in the Oxford English Dictionary. As one does? Until I stumbled onto this passage, I would have guessed that the middle of the Venn diagram of “people who use the paywalled OED rather than Dictionary.com” and “people who don’t know what cathartic means” is empty, like the Strategic Petroleum Reserve. I’m not saying that Vance’s story is impossible. I’m just saying that if it did happen as described, it would be like somebody discovering this Beethoven character in his well-thumbed set of the multivolume New Grove Dictionary of Music and Musicians.

If random disclosure of common-sense-defying detail is your idea of a searing, poignant journey into the light of faith, there’s more where that came from. Communion includes what is almost certainly the ne plus ultra of a new rhetorical trope—the posthumous invocation of Charlie Kirk to justify whatever the author or speaker wants you to believe or do. You know what I mean: the thing where a conservative podcast host assures his audience that a week or a few days or possibly just minutes before the man’s death, Kirk shared the secret gnosis about Israel, which is inevitably followed by a riposte from a Mar-a-Lago influencer insisting with equal stridency that milliseconds before (or perhaps even after) the shooting, she received private confirmation via text message of Kirk’s established public position. In this case, however, the spirit is summoned to explain why Usha had become pregnant again; specifically, we learn that Kirk’s widow, Erika, told Vance’s wife that she regrets not having more kids with Charlie, and that this moved Usha deeply and—Marvin Gaye needle drop.

People decide to have children for every possible reason or none. But until recently, the actual circumstances leading up to their conception were generally a private matter. If a dead friend inspired me and my wife to renew our acquaintance with the facts of life, I would probably not feel comfortable immortalizing his influence in my spiritual memoir slash campaign manifesto. But over and over again in these pages, Vance gives the impression that his book is meant to provide some kind of quasi-evangelical impetus to aspiring parents. If the right-wing dads of America start using “Let’s do it—for Charlie!” as a come-on, the U.S. birth rate is going to drop below that of Vatican City.

This oversharing of small details exists uneasily alongside a surprising lack of reflection on the book’s ostensible themes. A memoir that begins with criticism of anhedonic ambition becomes the chronicle of a man who, in less than a decade, is elected to the Senate and becomes vice president of the United States. Does everything that Vance says about the perfunctory pursuit of empty honors (he gives the example of Supreme Court clerkships) apply to his own rapid ascent to the office memorably described by John Nance Garner, one of its former holders, as “not worth a bucket of warm piss”? If not, why? Vance skates around this question—not, I think, because he is actively trying to do so, but because it actually seems not to have occurred to him.

There is one interesting exception here to Vance’s lack of reflection on his political career: a two-page apology for his remarks about “childless cat ladies.” For what it’s worth, I think he is protesting too much. When these comments came up again during the 2024 presidential campaign, my sister was a childless cat lady, a fan of Vance’s writing, and a Trump voter; she joked about his remarks with her husband, a naturalized citizen born in Mexico, and voted for Donald Trump again. Now the two of them are the proud parents of a beautiful baby boy. Only in America! At any rate, for me, this pang (OED : “a sudden sharp feeling of mental anguish or intense emotional pain”) of conscience was oddly precise in its calibration. If Vance now recognizes his comments as churlish, lacking in charity, and utterly unlikely to persuade voters, much less witness to the sublime truths of the Christian religion, good for him. But what about those Haitians in Ohio who, it turned out, were not barbecuing Lassie? Does he consider those dog whistles at least equally ill-conceived? Or was the issue simply that dunking on DINKs is a riskier gambit for graduates of Yale Law School than race-baiting?

I could go on. But to what end? I have said that after reading Communion, I was left wondering why it had been published. This is not as cutting a remark as it might sound. Even less interesting than a deeply mediocre book is a reviewer taking such a book too seriously. There is not a single person in the United States whose desire to read (or purchase) it will be affected by my opinions.

Could Vance have written a better book? Could Hard Choices have been better? Come on. When I say that Communion is a complete waste (of time, paper, the resources of the vice presidency, etc.), I really mean it, but I would say the same if I had been asked to review, say, Kamala Harris’s 107 Days. Here is a sample from what her ghostwriter expects us to believe is an actual conversation:

“I’m fully behind you, Joe,” I told him. “But if you decide not to run, I’m ready. And I would give it all I’ve got, because Trump has to be beaten.”

Dialogue is always stilted in these books. But for every ludicrous snippet of fanfic like this, there is at least an equal number of conversations so boring that they must be true. Early on in Jill Biden’s View From the East Wing, the former first lady recounts a conversation that begins “Let’s have lunch,” to which her husband replies, “Okay.” A few sentences later, the repartee continues, with the couple addressing a perhaps invisible server whose existence we are simply meant to infer (unless, of course, the Bidens were talking to themselves).

“Chardonnay, please,” I said.



Joe said, “I’ll have a Diet Coke.”



Before we’d even looked at the menu, I said:



“This is it, Joe. You have to make up your mind. Are you going to run or not?”



“Yes, I want to run,” he told me, matter-of-factly.



At last, clarity.



“Okay,” I said.

Okay. On what possible scale involving literary merit, human interest, or historical value is it even possible to evaluate a text containing these sentences?

At times the book reads like a bold linguistic experiment, an attempt to discover the absolute minimum level of evocation that can be achieved by what are technically grammatical English sentences. Don’t get me wrong, though. View From the East Wing would not have been improved if it had been more lyrical. The single worst thing about the book is that every few pages, we get a high-toned quotation, or at least an attempt at one: “The Danish philosopher Søren Kierkegaard said, ‘Life can only be understood backwards, but it must be lived forwards.’ ” He didn’t.

The only thing harder than envisioning the end of capitalism is trying to imagine a readable book written by (or attributed to) a contemporary American political figure. These books are not meant to be seriously reviewed, for the very simple reason that they are not meant to be seriously read. It is hardly an exaggeration to say that they are not meant to be read at all. They exist to be announced breathlessly, advertised, embargoed, analyzed via leaks and scooplets, discussed on podcasts—but their final end is to collect dust in the free pile outside used-book stores. For a laugh, go to the books section of a Salvation Army or a Goodwill and see how many copies of Bill Clinton’s My Life you can find. My personal record is 43, sitting side by side in their glorious order and array, like the blessed angels in heaven.

The problem here extends beyond books by politicians (and their wives). Already so much of what publishers find room for is “current affairs” slop that is basically indistinguishable from the kind of bad writing I am talking about, save for the fact that the authors are television stars or podcasters or—(shudders)—political reporters rather than elected officials. Only readers can stop this madness, by turning their attention elsewhere. Read about the past. Read about faraway places. Read Macaulay or Sylvia Townsend Warner or Santayana’s letters. Read a real book.

This article appears in the September 2026 print edition with the headline “A Book That Wasn’t Meant to Be Read.” When you buy a book using a link on this page, we receive a commission. Thank you for supporting The Atlantic.

entries.title: Why Widow’s Bay Is the Show of the Summer
entries.summary: Its blend of horror and comedy channels the spirit of the times.
entries.content:
Its blend of horror and comedy channels the spirit of the times.

What genre are we living in, living through, today? Epic? No chance. Farce? Sometimes. Porno-pastoral? Holistic satire? Digital picaresque? All of these, sure, but only in spasms. No, the big one, the all-encompassing super-genre of where we are right now (did you read/watch/inhale the news this morning?), is obvious: horror-comedy.

Horror (screams) is over here, and comedy (laughs) is over there. And exactly where we find ourselves on the dial that ticks between the two will vary from moment to moment. But the distance that separates them, in 2026 America, is not great; that’s the point. The ludicrous abuts the terrible. The ghastly now sits down heavily next to the hilarious, and breathes.

Which brings us to Widow’s Bay, currently riding high on Apple TV, by the former Parks and Recreation writer Katie Dippold. Not only does this show successfully blend the genres of horror and comedy; it reminds us that they are kind of the same thing—that horror was always wrapped around comedy, and vice versa, because they share the same brain stem. Sitcom-style no-exit environments; slapstick violence; the alternating rhythm of anxiety and release. The jump scare, the punch line. The existential whiplash. And what greater monster, what deeper bump in the night, than the possibility that all of this is, you know, just one big joke?

Widow’s Bay is an island off the coast of New England. It has a diner called the Driftwood, some nice beaches, some craggy old fishermen, no cellphone service, and no Wi‑Fi. The lack of hooked-upness feels important: It is as if the island’s imagination, absent the usual distractions and numbing agents, has gone into blooming-and-festering psychotic overdrive. Everywhere you look there’s a curse or a mystery or a bottomless backstory, and the whole place creaks and slithers with that special Stephen King–like aura of hexed quaintness, of bed-and-breakfast menace.

Widow’s Bay has a mayor, Tom Loftis, who wants to turn it into the new Martha’s Vineyard. Loftis, played by Matthew Rhys, is a figure of quietly fanatical secularism and non-ghost-ism. Clear out the cobwebs and crank up the cappuccino machine. But it is his one-man campaign of gentrification and municipal enhancement that will activate the demons of the island. He whose blundering-about like Michael Scott will bring in the evil fog. He whose desire for summer visitors—and impatience with any kind of supernatural nonsense—will wake up the killer clown and the lurching slasher and the dripping, lank-haired sea hag, who follows you home and sits horribly and brinily on your face until you’re dead. “You refuse to accept our history, to accept the truth!” rails Wyck the fisherman, the wild-eyed town doomsayer. “Do you know what I think?” says Loftis. “I think there are some people who want to be miserable.”

Rhys as Loftis is expertly cast. Bloodless complexion, inky hair—a great silent-movie face, I’ve always thought, a melodramatic black-and-white face, with something nervously mobile and Chaplin-esque flickering around the mouth, the bared-teeth smile. The tonal coherence of Widow’s Bay depends to a large extent on Rhys, on his ability to ride the current between goofy startlement and raw terror. Loftis lives with, and glumly rinses out the cereal bowls of, his rebellious stoner son, Evan—an extraordinary performance by Kingston Rumi Southwick, full of hazily disruptive rhythms and long, glossy, bullshit-detecting gazes. “I didn’t know we were swearing now,” he says placidly, having provoked his father into a burst of addled profanity.

Then there’s Patricia, played by the English actor Kate O’Flynn. Awkward, traumatized, alarming on the dance floor, doggedly writing her poems and trying to make friends, in horror she’s a victim and in comedy she’s a heroine. So on the horror-comedy island of Widow’s Bay she’s both, embellishing how she almost got murdered during a spree by the aforementioned lurching slasher. Remember all those fake memoirs from the early 2000s, by all those writers pretending to be addicts and gangsters and Holocaust survivors? Patricia could have written one of them. “I still sleep with a dresser in front of my door,” she insists. “But he murdered teenage girls,” Loftis says helpfully. “You’re in your 40s; you’ll be fine.” “Beach Reads,” the episode in which Patricia organizes a cocktail party based on the guidance of a slightly sinister self-help/etiquette manual (“Feeling like you could die tomorrow and no one would notice? Well, that could be. But the right party can change that.”), is a stand-alone classic: When the veil of hallucination lifts, the manual is revealed to be a Blair Witch–style grimoire.

It’s very well-made, Widow’s Bay. The soundtrack—by the frequent Hans Zimmer collaborator David Fleming—is a delight, a polyphonic mood board of ghost-clangs, spooky bassoons, lyrical electro-motifs, and sighing sea themes. And some of the images are indelible: A close-up of Rhys prone and horror-rigid in his bathtub, the watery essence of the sea hag hovering over his face, will not soon be dislodged from my mind.

The risk, though, with horror-comedy—with dinging from chuckles to shrieks and back again—is always an evacuated center. A lack of feeling at the core.

The masterpieces of the genre have the means to overcome this. Edgar Wright’s Shaun of the Dead is more chuckles than shrieks, but it makes a serious metaphysical statement: If you let your life drift, if you sit in the pub all day, if you don’t self-actualize, the zombies are going to get you—the zombie tide will take you down. Jordan Peele’s Get Out is more shrieks than chuckles, but some of its creepiest moments occur within a brilliantly observed comedy of manners, as Chris, a young Black man, is introduced to his girlfriend’s preeningly progressive white parents, and begins to be drawn into the sticky web of their whiteness. (“So how long has this been going on?” leers the dad about his daughter’s relationship. “This—this thang.”) As for Twin Peaks … Well, Twin Peaks is Twin Peaks. Nobody does it like David Lynch, bringing such a pressure of seething strangeness to bear on both the chuckles and the shrieks that a third element was created, a third genre: the Lynchian.

Season 1 of Widow’s Bay does not operate at this level. The final showdown with the island’s dark past gives you not catharsis but that more familiar groggy, post-binge, hunched-over-your-laptop feeling, your brain glutted with narrative and your antennae drooping. But a second season is in the works, with the potential to deepen the action, and the world will reliably continue to keep us in the mood: more horror, more comedy. Nobody’s getting off this island yet.

This article appears in the September 2026 print edition with the headline “Not Quite the Vineyard.”

entries.title: Pete Hegseth, You Call That a Pull-Up?
entries.summary: Military fitness experts say the secretary of defense fails on form.
entries.content:
Military fitness experts say the secretary of defense fails on form.

Pete Hegseth wants us to watch him sweat. Videos of the secretary of defense in a T-shirt and shorts working out alongside military personnel have become a trademark of his tenure. He has been filmed running on Omaha Beach, swinging a kettlebell on a university campus, and doing jumping jacks on an amphibious assault ship. In one clip, Hegseth pumps iron with troops at the Guantánamo Bay naval base—or, as a Defense Department X account put it, “America’s @SECWAR CRUSHED 44 reps on the bench after a morning run.”

But did he really crush those reps? Each time a new episode of Boot Camp Pete drops, critics skewer his form and mock his endurance. They insist that he’s all flex and no fundamentals. In that bench-press clip, for example, he doesn’t fully extend his arms, making the exercise far easier and inflating his total. A few seconds into the clip, you can hear someone yell, “All the way down, all the way up!” Hegseth fails to heed that helpful cue.

Such nitpicking would be uncharitable if Hegseth were just another 40-something dad getting a lift in at Planet Fitness. But these are highlight reels, posted by the Pentagon. Plus, the former Fox News host isn’t exactly known for withholding judgment himself: Recall how, last September, he gathered the nation’s generals and admirals together so that he could upbraid them for being overweight. He told them that leaders set the standard and that when “you let the small stuff go, the big stuff eventually goes.”

With Hegseth’s wisdom in mind, let’s examine what the United States military’s training manuals have to say about proper exercise form. Short answer: a lot. You’ll find hundreds of pages spelling out precisely how to perform all manner of movements, including how to stretch your thighs (“grasp the left ankle and pull toward the left buttock”), how to plank (“forearms flat on the ground, forming a triangle”), and how to throw a four-pound ball from a seated position (“do not throw the medicine ball like shooting a basketball”). These manuals are replete with charts, bullet points, and illustrations. The Army’s Holistic Health and Fitness Testing manual provides detailed instructions, accompanied by photographs, on the bench press, warning that you must not “jerk or shrug the shoulders, arch the back, or allow the hips to rise off the bench” (note to Secretary Hegseth: p. 65, Appendix B).

Standards vary across branches, even for basic exercises. Take the push-up. For the Army’s fitness test, soldiers must do what’s known as a hand-release push-up, requiring them to stick their arms straight out to the side after each repetition. The Marine guide devotes several hundred words to describing how to do a traditional push-up, specifying that the chest may not rest on the deck “except as required to ensure that the upper arms are at least parallel to the deck.” In the Air Force, your feet cannot be more than 12 inches apart, and you must maintain a “rigid head to heel form.” These are directives rather than suggestions. Failure to meet the standard results in a “no-go,” in military parlance, meaning you haven’t passed that portion of the test.

Hegseth’s push-ups wouldn’t pass muster in any branch. Last summer, Hegseth and Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. kicked off the “Pete & Bobby Challenge,” in which participants attempt to do 50 pull-ups and 100 push-ups in less than 10 minutes. Hegseth’s push-ups are more like half-ups: In the ones captured on video, he doesn’t go through the full range of motion. His substandard technique is ironic because he’s on record ridiculing others for their lack of push-up prowess. In his 2024 book, The War on Warriors, Hegseth mocks women soldiers who can do only “ten push-ups at a pop” and refers to a transgender woman as a “ten-push-up kind of guy.”

To be fair, Hegseth’s pull-up form, at times, is more or less textbook. The issue is consistency: Hegseth can’t seem to maintain the standard for the duration of the competition, and raises his knees to his chest to give himself additional oomph. This is known as “kipping” and is forbidden in an eight-page pull-up guide published by the Marines. In one clip, Hegseth kips while using an underhand grip, which technically makes what he’s doing a chin-up—an exercise often considered less challenging than a pull-up. (The Marine guide permits both, though kipping is always forbidden.) According to the guide: “Poor quality work will prevent you from getting the most from your workout and can lead to injury.” Hegseth’s work is, by Marine standards, poor.

Hegseth claims at the end of the video that he finished in less than six minutes, which would be impressive if he weren’t flouting the rules and sacrificing form for speed. What’s more, he’s doing these exercises incorrectly during a challenge that bears his name. Given all of this, it’s impossible to have confidence in the validity of Hegseth’s self-reported feat.

Granted, I am not a trained grader of military exercises. Prior to perusing these manuals, I wasn’t aware that your arms need to be at a 45-degree angle in relation to your trunk for the modified bent-leg body twist, or that it is imperative to point your toes to the rear for the prone row. I was entirely unfamiliar with the squat bender and the quadraplex. Nor did I know, before watching an Army instructional video, how to swing a kettlebell.

As it happens, neither does Hegseth, who, in January, was filmed swinging a kettlebell with recruits at UCLA. You’re supposed to keep the kettlebell near your body and generate momentum with your legs and hips. Instead, Hegseth squats, as if doing deep knee bends, and brings the weight far too close to the ground, putting strain on his shoulders and back. This is wrong, according to Joel Del Rosario, a retired Marine and a kettlebell coach. Del Rosario told me he’s glad to see Hegseth encouraging better fitness in the military, and he noted that, in his experience, pretty much everyone who gives the kettlebell a whirl fails at first. He said he’d be honored if asked to show Hegseth how to do the exercise properly.

I consulted other military exercise experts too. Among them was Stew Smith, a former Navy SEAL and the author of a series of books about prepping to qualify for the Special Forces, including one on the Ranger Physical Assessment. A former Army National Guard infantry officer, Hegseth has bemoaned slipping standards across the military, including at the Ranger School—even though he never completed the famously grueling 61-day leadership and fitness course. Smith said he doesn’t evaluate the performance of people unless they ask, and so wouldn’t comment specifically on Hegseth’s approach and abilities. But he said he stresses to his students the importance of going all the way up and all the way down, whether it’s a pull-up, push-up, or bench press.

I also asked Maureen LeBoeuf what she made of the “Pete & Bobby Challenge” video. LeBoeuf is a retired brigadier general and the first and only woman to head the physical-education department at West Point, a position known as “master of the sword.” She said that nothing she saw from the defense secretary in that video would have counted if she had been the grader, and she has graded thousands of such tests. “His pull-ups or chin-ups and the kicking that he does—you would never allow that. You’d stop somebody; you’d say, ‘Okay, you’re done,’ ” LeBoeuf said. “He just keeps kicking, and it’s not a good look.” She allowed that he might have been exhausted in those clips, but the Army doesn’t usually recognize fatigue as an excuse. And it’s hard to forgive such lapses and corner-cutting from someone who, as LeBoeuf put it, is “real big on standards.”

In response to an inquiry about the criticism of Hegseth’s workouts, Joel Valdez, the acting Pentagon press secretary, insisted that Hegseth was being unfairly singled out. “Have you ever seen Lloyd Austin’s form? Did the Atlantic ever ask about it?” he wrote. “The answer to both of those questions is no.” That’s true, but Austin, the previous secretary of defense, was not known for posting self-promotional exercise content on social media. He did, however, complete Ranger School and Airborne School, command the 10th Mountain Division in Afghanistan, lead the fight against the Islamic State, and earn a Silver Star.

This article appears in the September 2026 print edition with the headline “You Call That a Pull-Up?”

entries.title: The Canoeist Accused of Destroying the Reflecting Pool
entries.summary: The Olympian has become the latest emblem of possible prosecutorial overreach.
entries.content:
The Olympian has become the latest emblem of possible prosecutorial overreach.

The summer after the country celebrated its 200th birthday, David Hearn paddled a canoe nicknamed the Queen Mary around the Reflecting Pool as part of an event intended to draw attention to a sport that would later lead him to Olympic glory. Nearly half a century later, as the country approached its 250th birthday, Hearn was back. On June 19, the 67-year-old stopped by the pool at the tail end of a long bike ride to look at the floating flakes of sealant that had come untethered following a rushed renovation ordered by President Trump. Hearn says he snapped some photos and dipped his hand into the water. Then he was arrested. Officers put him in handcuffs and detained him for hours.

Federal prosecutors have accused Hearn of “forcefully and violently” ripping up the sealant, causing damage to federal property that should be punishable by up to 10 years in prison. Hearn’s lawyers argue that these are ridiculous and frivolous charges—and in a motion to dismiss the case said they could present photographic evidence proving he didn’t cause major damage. Hearn, who for many years sold waterproofing materials for boats and canoes, has suggested that he was trying to figure out why the pool’s lining was falling apart, not damage it.

If last year’s emblem of prosecutorial overreach was the Sandwich Guy, this year’s has become the Canoe Guy.

Abbie Endicott, who helped organize the canoe event 49 years ago, told me that the Reflecting Pool holds special meaning to her, Hearn, and the others who paddled the shallow water that day. “The idea that any of us would want to damage something that’s meant to be so beautiful—and is, when it’s clean,” said Endicott, whose husband, Bill, was a longtime head coach of the U.S. canoeing team. “It’s meant to reflect everything that’s around it.”

With so many headlines to choose from, it’s a little strange that the Reflecting Pool has become the political scandal of the summer. But it has a dash of everything that Trump’s critics loathe about this presidency: a vanity project driven by Trump’s desire to accomplish what his predecessors couldn’t; a no-bid contract for a straight-from-central-casting Trump donor; incompetence leading to a botched repair; refusals to acknowledge reality; and, finally, the scapegoating of perceived enemies. Along the way, algae bloomed, chemicals were dumped, and ducks turned up dead. The banks of the pool—at its best a scene of unparalleled American majesty—took on a sort of circus atmosphere as locals and tourists alike stopped by to see the disaster for themselves. Some took a small souvenir to commemorate the moment.

The day after Hearn’s arrest, law enforcement from around the country, who had just arrived to help with the July 4 celebrations, swarmed the area. Some were assigned foot patrols near the Reflecting Pool and in quick succession, court records show, arrested several other visitors for allegedly reaching into the pool and pulling up pieces of sealant. On June 20 around 12:30 p.m., 47-year-old Justin Carreno was detained by a police officer from Oklahoma City.
